title: SegFormer: Simple and Efficient Design for Semantic Segmentation with Transformers

paragraphs.text:
## **SegFormer: Simple and Efficient Design for Semantic Segmentation with Transformers**

**Enze Xie**[1] **Wenhai Wang**[2] **Zhiding Yu**[3] _[∗]_ **Anima Anandkumar**[3] _[,]_[4] **Jose M. Alvarez**[3] **Ping Luo**[1] 1The University of Hong Kong 2Nanjing University 3NVIDIA 4Caltech

xieenze@hku.hk, wangwenhai362@163.com,

{zhidingy,josea,aanandkumar}@nvidia.com, pluo@cs.hku.hk

## **Abstract**

We present SegFormer, a simple, efficient yet powerful semantic segmentation framework which unifies Transformers with lightweight multilayer perceptron (MLP) decoders. SegFormer has two appealing features: 1) SegFormer comprises a novel hierarchically structured Transformer encoder which outputs multiscale features. It does not need positional encoding, thereby avoiding the interpolation of positional codes which leads to decreased performance when the testing resolution differs from training. 2) SegFormer avoids complex decoders. The proposed MLP decoder aggregates information from different layers, and thus combining both local attention and global attention to render powerful representations. We show that this simple and lightweight design is the key to efficient segmentation on Transformers. We scale our approach up to obtain a series of models from SegFormer-B0 to SegFormer-B5, reaching significantly better performance and efficiency than previous counterparts. For example, SegFormer-B4 achieves 50.3% mIoU on ADE20K with 64M parameters, being 5 _×_ smaller and 2.2% better than the previous best method. Our best model, SegFormer-B5, achieves 84.0% mIoU on Cityscapes validation set and shows excellent zero-shot robustness on Cityscapes-C. Code is available at: `github` _._ `com` _/_ `NVlabs` _/_ `SegFormer` .

## **1 Introduction**

Semantic segmentation is a fundamental task in computer vision and enables many downstream applications. It is related to image classification since it produces per-pixel category prediction instead of image-level prediction. This relationship is pointed out and systematically studied in a seminal work [1], where the authors used fully convolutional networks (FCNs) for semantic segmentation tasks. Since then, FCN has inspired many follow-up works and has become a predominant design choice for dense prediction.

Since there is a strong relation between classification and semantic segmentation, many stateof-the-art semantic segmentation frameworks are variants of popular architectures for image classification on ImageNet. Therefore, designing backbone architectures has remained an active area

in semantic segmentation. Indeed, starting from

early methods using VGGs [1, 2], to the latest methods with significantly deeper and more powerful backbones [3], the evolution of backbones has dramatically pushed the performance boundary of semantic segmentation. Besides backbone architectures, another line of work formulates semantic segmentation as a structured prediction problem, and focuses on designing modules and operators, which can effectively capture contextual information. A representative example in this area is dilated convolution [4, 5], which increases the receptive field by “inflating” the kernel with holes.

Witnessing the great success in natural language processing (NLP), there has been a recent surge of interest to introduce Transformers to vision tasks. Dosovitskiy et al. [6] proposed vision Transformer (ViT) for image classification. Following the Transformer design in NLP, the authors split an image into multiple linearly embedded patches and feed them into a standard Transformer with positional embeddings (PE), leading to an impressive performance on ImageNet. In semantic segmentation, Zheng et al. [7] proposed SETR to demonstrate the feasibility of using Transformers in this task.

SETR adopts ViT as a backbone and incorporates several CNN decoders to enlarge feature resolution. Despite the good performance, ViT has two important limitations: 1) ViT outputs single-scale lowresolution features instead of multi-scale ones, and 2) it has very high computational cost on large images. To address these limitations, Wang et al. [8] proposed a pyramid vision Transformer (PVT), a natural extension of ViT with pyramid structures for dense prediction. PVT shows considerable improvements over the ResNet counterpart on object detection and semantic segmentation. However, together with other emerging methods such as Swin Transformer [9] and Twins [10], these methods mainly consider the design of the Transformer encoder, neglecting the contribution of the decoder for further improvements.

This paper introduces SegFormer, a cutting-edge Transformer framework for semantic segmentation that jointly considers efficiency, accuracy, and robustness. In contrast to previous methods, our framework redesigns both the encoder and the decoder. The key novelties of our approach are:

- A novel positional-encoding-free and hierarchical Transformer encoder.

- A lightweight All-MLP decoder design that yields a powerful representation without complex and computationally demanding modules.
- As shown in Figure 1, SegFormer sets new a state-of-the-art in terms of efficiency, accuracy and robustness in three publicly available semantic segmentation datasets.

First, the proposed encoder avoids interpolating positional codes when performing inference on images with resolutions different from the training one. As a result, our encoder can easily adapt to arbitrary test resolutions without impacting the performance. In addition, the hierarchical part enables the encoder to generate both high-resolution fine features and low-resolution coarse features, this is in contrast to ViT that can only produce single low-resolution feature maps with fixed resolutions. Second, we propose a lightweight MLP decoder where the key idea is to take advantage of the Transformer-induced features where the attentions of lower layers tend to stay local, whereas the ones of the highest layers are highly non-local. By aggregating the information from different layers, the MLP decoder combines both local and global attention. As a result, we obtain a simple and straightforward decoder that renders powerful representations.

We demonstrate the advantages of SegFormer in terms of model size, run-time, and accuracy on three publicly available datasets: ADE20K, Cityscapes, and COCO-Stuff. On Citysapces, our lightweight model, SegFormer-B0, without accelerated implementations such as TensorRT, yields 71.9% mIoU at 48 FPS, which, compared to ICNet [11], represents a relative improvement of 60% and 4.2% in latency and performance, respectively. Our largest model, SegFormer-B5, yields 84.0% mIoU, which represents a relative 1.8% mIoU improvement while being 5 _×_ faster than SETR [7]. On ADE20K, this model sets a new state-of-the-art of 51.8% mIoU while being 4 _×_ smaller than SETR. Moreover, our approach is significantly more robust to common corruptions and perturbations than existing methods, therefore being suitable for safety-critical applications. Code will be publicly available.

## **2 Related Work**

**Semantic Segmentation.** Semantic segmentation can be seen as an extension of image classification from image level to pixel level. In the deep learning era [12–14], FCN [1] is the fundamental work of semantic segmentation, which is a fully convolution network that performs pixel-to-pixel classification in an end-to-end manner. After that, researchers focused on improving FCN from different aspects such as: enlarging the receptive field [15–17, 5, 2, 4, 18]; refining the contextual information [19– 27]; introducing boundary information [28–35]; designing various attention modules [36–44]; or using AutoML technologies [45–49]. These methods significantly improve semantic segmentation performance at the expense of introducing many empirical modules, making the resulting framework computationally demanding and complicated. More recent methods have proved the effectiveness of Transformer-based architectures for semantic segmentation [7, 44]. However, these methods are still computationally demanding.

**Transformer backbones** . ViT [6] is the first work to prove that a pure Transformer can achieve state-of-the-art performance in image classification. ViT treats each image as a sequence of tokens and then feeds them to multiple Transformer layers to make the classification. Subsequently, DeiT [50] further explores a data-efficient training strategy and a distillation approach for ViT. More recent methods such as T2T ViT [51], CPVT [52], TNT [53], CrossViT [54] and LocalViT [55] introduce tailored changes to ViT to further improve image classification performance.

Beyond classification, PVT [8] is the first work to introduce a pyramid structure in Transformer, demonstrating the potential of a pure Transformer backbone compared to CNN counterparts in dense prediction tasks. After that, methods such as Swin [9], CvT [56], CoaT [57], LeViT [58] and Twins [10] enhance the local continuity of features and remove fixed size position embedding to improve the performance of Transformers in dense prediction tasks.

**Transformers for specific tasks** . DETR [50] is the first to use Transformers for end-to-end object detection framework without non-maximum suppression (NMS). Other works have also used Transformers in tasks such as tracking [59, 60], super-resolution [61], re-id [62], colorization [63], retrieval [64] and multi-modal learning [65, 66]. For semantic segmentation, SETR [7] adopts ViT [6] as a backbone to extract features, achieving impressive performance. However, these Transformer-based methods have very low efficiency and, thus, difficult to deploy in real-time applications.

## **3 Method**

As depicted in Figure 2, SegFormer consists of two main modules: (1) a hierarchical Transformer encoder; and (2) a lightweight All-MLP decoder to predict the final mask. Given an image with size _H × W ×_ 3, we first divide it into patches of size 4 _×_ 4. Unlike ViT which uses 16 _×_ 16, using fine-grained patches favors semantic segmentation. Second, we use these patches as input to the hierarchical Transformer encoder to get multi-level features with resolution {1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32}

3

of the original image. We then pass these multi-level features to the All-MLP decoder to predict the segmentation mask with a _[H]_ 4 _[×][W]_ 4 _[×][ N][cls]_[resolution, where] _[ N][cls]_[is the number of categories.] In the remainder of this section, we first detail the proposed encoder and decoder designs and then summarize the main differences of our approach compared to SETR.

## **3.1 Hierarchical Transformer Encoder**

We design a series of Mix Transformer encoders (MiT), MiT-B0 to MiT-B5, with the same architecture but different sizes. On top of the hierarchical architecture and efficient self-attention module in PVT [8], we further propose several novel features including **overlapped patch merging** and **positional-encoding-free design** which will be shown to greatly benefit the segmentation tasks.

**Hierarchical Feature Representation.** Unlike ViT [6], our encoder generates multi-level multi-scale features given an input image. These features provide both high-resolution coarse features and lowresolution fine-grained features that boost the performance of semantic segmentation. Specifically, given an input image with size _H × W ×_ 3, we perform patch merging to obtain a hierarchical feature map _Fi_ with a resolution of 2 _[i] H_[+1] _[×]_ 2 _W[i]_[+1] _[×][ C][i]_[, where] _[ i][ ∈{]_[1] _[,]_[ 2] _[,]_[ 3] _[,]_[ 4] _[}]_[, and] _[ C][i]_[+1][ is larger than] _[ C][i]_[.]

**Overlapped Patch Merging.** Given an image patch, the patch merging process used in ViT, unifies a _N × N ×_ 3 patch into a 1 _×_ 1 _× C_ vector. This can easily be extended to unify a 2 _×_ 2 _× Ci_ feature path into a 1 _×_ 1 _× Ci_ +1 vector to obtain hierarchical feature maps. Using this, we can shrink our hierarchical features from _F_ 1 ( _[H]_ 4 _[×][W]_ 4 _[×][ C]_[1][) to] _[ F]_[2][(] _[H]_ 8 _[×][W]_ 8 _[×][ C]_[2][), and then iterate for any] other feature map in the hierarchy. This process was initially designed to combine non-overlapping image or feature patches. Therefore, it fails to preserve the local continuity around those patches. Instead, we use an overlapping patch merging process. To this end, we define _K_ , _S_ , and _P_ , where _K_ is the patch size, _S_ is the stride between two adjacent patches, and _P_ is the padding size. In our experiments, we set _K_ = 7, _S_ = 4, _P_ = 3 ,and _K_ = 3, _S_ = 2, _P_ = 1 to perform overlapping patch merging to produces features with the same size as the non-overlapping process. Similar to the original patch embedding in ViT [6], this operation can be implemented by “nn.Conv2D” in PyTorch.

**Positional-Encoding-Free Design.** The resolution of the PE in ViT is fixed. One thus needs to interpolate the PE when the test resolution differs from training. This leads to the drop of accuracy, which is undesirable since the resolution mismatch is common in semantic segmentation. We instead introduce Mix-FFN where we consider the effect of zero padding to the leak location information [67] by directly using a 3 _×_ 3 Conv in the feed-forward network (FFN). Mix-FFN is formulated as:

where **x** _in_ is the feature from the self-attention module. Mix-FFN mixes a 3 _×_ 3 convolution and an MLP into each FFN. In our experiments, we will show that a 3 _×_ 3 convolution is sufficient to provide positional information for Transformers. In particular, we use depth-wise convolutions for reducing the number of parameters and improving efficiency.

4

It should be mentioned that CPVT [52] also alleviates this issue by using a 3 _×_ 3 Conv to generate conditional PE at different resolutions and then add it to the feature map. Our work conceptually goes one step further as we argue that adding PE to feature map is not necessary in semantic segmentation. Another recent work CvT [56] introduced 3 _×_ 3 Convs to model the spatial relationship among tokens. Despite the converging design, our work differs in both motivation and application as we aim to totally remove PEs to handle the training/testing resolution mismatch issue in semantic segmentation. Our intuition started from [67] whereas the same intuition was not discussed in CvT.

## **3.2 Lightweight All-MLP Decoder**

SegFormer incorporates a lightweight decoder consisting only of MLP layers and this avoiding the hand-crafted and computationally demanding components typically used in other methods. The key to enabling such a simple decoder is that our hierarchical Transformer encoder has a larger effective receptive field (ERF) than traditional CNN encoders.

The proposed All-MLP decoder consists of four main steps. First, multi-level features _Fi_ from the MiT encoder go through an MLP layer to unify the channel dimension. Then, in a second step, features are up-sampled to 1/4th and concatenated together. Third, a MLP layer is adopted to fuse the concatenated features _F_ . Finally, another MLP layer takes the fused feature to predict the segmentation mask _M_ with a _[H]_ 4 _[×][W]_ 4 _[×][ N][cls]_[resolution, where] _[ N][cls]_[is the number of categories.] This lets us formulate the decoder as:
where M refers to the predicted mask, and Linear( _Cin, Cout_ )( _·_ ) refers to a linear layer with _Cin_ and _Cout_ as input and output vector dimensions respectively.

**Effective Receptive Field Analysis.** For semantic segmentation, maintaining large receptive field to include context information has been a central issue [5, 17, 18]. Here, we use effective receptive field (ERF) [68] as a toolkit to visualize and interpret why our MLP decoder design is so effective on Transformers. In Figure 3, we visualize ERFs of the four encoder stages and the decoder heads for both DeepLabv3+ and SegFormer. We can make the following observations:

- The ERF of DeepLabv3+ is relatively small even at Stage-4, the deepest stage.

- SegFormer’s encoder naturally produces local attentions which resemble convolutions at lower stages, while able to output highly non-local attentions that effectively capture contexts at Stage-4.
- As shown with the zoom-in patches in Figure 3, the ERF of the MLP head (blue box) differs from Stage-4 (red box) with a significant stronger local attention besides the non-local attention.

The limited receptive field in CNN requires one to resort to context modules such as ASPP [16] that enlarge the receptive field but inevitably become heavy. Our decoder design benefits from the non-local attention in Transformers and leads to a larger receptive field without being complex. The same decoder design, however, does not work well on CNN backbones since the overall receptive field is upper bounded by the limited one at Stage-4, and we will verify this later in Table 1d,

More importantly, our decoder design essentially takes advantage of a Transformer induced feature that produces both highly local and non-local attention at the same time. By unifying them, our MLP decoder renders complementary and powerful representations by adding few parameters. This is

5

another key reason that motivated our design. Taking the non-local attention from Stage-4 alone is not enough to produce good results, as will be verified in Table 1d.

## **3.3 Relationship to SETR.**

SegFormer contains multiple more efficient and powerful designs compared with SETR [7]:

- We only use ImageNet-1K for pre-training. ViT in SETR is pre-trained on larger ImageNet-22K.

- SegFormer’s encoder has a hierarchical architecture, which is smaller than ViT and can capture both high-resolution coarse and low-resolution fine features. In contrast, SETR’s ViT encoder can only generate single low-resolution feature map.
- We remove Positional Embedding in encoder, while SETR uses fixed shape Positional Embedding which decreases the accuracy when the resolution at inference differs from the training ones.

- Our MLP decoder is more compact and less computationally demanding than the one in SETR. This leads to a _negligible_ computational overhead. In contrast, SETR requires heavy decoders with multiple 3 _×_ 3 convolutions.

## **4 Experiments**

## **4.1 Experimental Settings**

> **Datasets:** We used four public datasets: Cityscapes [69], ADE20K [70], and COCO-Stuff [71]. ADE20K is a dataset covering 150 fine-grained semantic concepts consisting of 20210 images. Cityscapes is a driving dataset for semantic segmentation consisting of 5000 fine-annotated high resolution images with 19 categories. COCO-Stuff covers 172 labels and consists of 164k images: 118k for training, 5k for validation, 20k for test-dev and 20k for the test-challenge.

**Implementation details:** We used the _mmsegmentation_[2] codebase and train on a server with 8 Tesla V100. We pre-train the encoder on the Imagenet-1K dataset and randomly initialize the decoder. During training, we applied data augmentation through random resize with ratio 0.5-2.0, random horizontal flipping, and random cropping to 512 _×_ 512, 1024 _×_ 1024, 512 _×_ 512 for ADE20K, Cityscapes and COCO-Stuff. Following [9] we set crop size to 640 _×_ 640 on ADE20K for our largest model B5. We trained the models using AdamW optimizer for 160K iterations on ADE20K, Cityscapes, and 80K iterations on COCO-Stuff. Exceptionally, for the ablation studies, we trained the models for 40K iterations. We used a batch size of 16 for ADE20K, COCO-Stuff and a batch size of 8 for Cityscapes. The learning rate was set to an initial value of 0.00006 and then used a “poly” LR schedule with factor 1.0 by default. For simplicity, we _did not_ adopt widely-used tricks such as OHEM, auxiliary losses or class balance loss. During evaluation, we rescale the short side of the image to training cropping size and keep the aspect ratio for ADE20K and COCO-Stuff. For Cityscapes, we do inference using sliding window test by cropping 1024 _×_ 1024 windows. We report semantic segmentation performance using mean Intersection over Union (mIoU).

## **4.2 Ablation Studies**

**Influence of the size of model.** We first analyze the effect of increasing the size of the encoder on the performance and model efficiency. Figure 1 shows the performance vs. model efficiency for ADE20K as a function of the encoder size and, Table 1a summarizes the results for the three datasets. The first thing to observe here is the size of the decoder compared to the encoder. As shown, for the lightweight model, the decoder has only 0.4M parameters. For MiT-B5 encoder, the decoder only takes up to 4% of the total number of parameters in the model. In terms of performance, we can observe that, overall, increasing the size of the encoder yields consistent improvements on all the datasets. Our lightweight model, SegFormer-B0, is compact and efficient while maintaining a competitive performance, showing that our method is very convenient for real-time applications. On the other hand, our SegFormer-B5, the largest model, achieves state-of-the-art results on all three datasets, showing the potential of our Transformer encoder.

**Influence of** _C_ **, the MLP decoder channel dimension.** We now analyze the influence of the channel dimension _C_ in the MLP decoder, see Section 3.2. In Table 1b we show performance, flops, and parameters as a function of this dimension. We can observe that setting _C_ = 256 provides a very competitive performance and computational cost. The performance increases as _C_ increases; however, it leads to larger and less efficient models. Interestingly, this performance plateaus for channel dimensions wider than 768. Given these results, we choose _C_ = 256 for our real-time models SegFormer-B0, B1 and _C_ = 768 for the rest.

**Mix-FFN vs. Positional Encoder (PE).** In this experiment, we analyze the effect of removing the positional encoding in the Transformer encoder in favor of using the proposed Mix-FFN. To this end, we train Transformer encoders with a positional encoding (PE) and the proposed Mix-FFN and perform inference on Cityscapes with two different image resolutions: 768 _×_ 768 using a sliding window, and 1024 _×_ 2048 using the whole image.

Table 1c shows the results for this experiment. As shown, for a given resolution, our approach using Mix-FFN clearly outperforms using a positional encoding. Moreover, our approach is less sensitive to differences in the test resolution: the accuracy drops 3.3% when using a positional encoding with a

7

lower resolution. In contrast, when we use the proposed Mix-FFN the performance drop is reduced to only 0.7%. From these results, we can conclude using the proposed Mix-FFN produces better and more robust encoders than those using positional encoding.

**Effective receptive field evaluation.** In Section 3.2, we argued that our MLP decoder benefits from Transformers having a larger effective receptive field compared to other CNN models. To quantify this effect, in this experiment, we compare the performance of our MLP-decoder when used with CNN-based encoders such as ResNet or ResNeXt. As shown in Table 1d, coupling our MLP-decoder with a CNN-based encoder yields a significantly lower accuracy compared to coupling it with the proposed Transformer encoder. Intuitively, as a CNN has a smaller receptive field than the Transformer (see the analysis in Section 3.2), the MLP-decoder is not enough for global reasoning. In contrast, coupling our Transformer encoder with the MLP decoder leads to the best performance. Moreover, for Transformer encoder, it is necessary to combine low-level local features and high-level non-local features instead of only high-level feature.

**Influence of difference encoders.** We select 2 representative Transformer encoders, ViT [6] and Swin [9] and compare with our MiT encoder. As shown in Table 3, with same decoder, _e.g._ MLP decoder, MiT-B2 is 3.1% higher than Swin-T with similar encoder parameters. Moreover, MiT-B5 has much fewer encoder parameters than ViT-large, but is 3+% mIoU higher than ViT-large. These experiments shows our MiT encoder is better than Swin and ViT for semantic segmentataion.

**Influence of difference decoders.** We also test MiT encoder with different decoders. As shown

in Table 3, the mIoUs are similar with different decoders while the proposed MLP decoder has the least parameters and is only 1 _/_ 8 of the UperNet decoder in Swin. The MLP decoder is thus an important design towards efficient segmentation.

## **4.3 Comparison to state of the art methods**

We now compare our results with existing approaches on the ADE20K [70] and Cityscapes [69]. More experiments about COCO-Stuff [71] are in appendix.

**ADE20K and Cityscapes:** Table 2 summarizes our results including parameters, FLOPS, latency, and accuracy for ADE20K and Cityscapes. In the top part of the table, we report real-time approaches where we include state-of-the-art methods and our results using the MiT-B0 lightweight encoder. In the bottom part, we focus on performance and report the results of our approach and related works using stronger encoders.

On ADE20K, SegFormer-B0 yields 37.4% mIoU using only 3.8M parameters and 8.4G FLOPs, outperforming all other real-time counterparts in terms of parameters, flops, and latency. For instance, compared to DeeplabV3+ (MobileNetV2), SegFormer-B0 is 7.4 FPS, which is faster and keeps 3.4% better mIoU.

Moreover, SegFormer-B5 outperforms all other approaches, including the previous best SETR, and establishes a new state-of-the-art of 51.8%, which is 1.6% mIoU better than SETR while being significantly more efficient.

As also shown in Table 2, our results also hold on Cityscapes. SegFormer-B0 yields 15.2 FPS and 76.2% mIoU (the shorter side of input image being 1024), which represents a 1.3% mIoU improvement and a 2 _×_ speedup compared to DeeplabV3+. Moreover, with the shorter side of input image being 512, SegFormer-B0 runs at 47.6 FPS and yields 71.9% mIoU, which is 17.3 FPS faster and 4.2% better than ICNet. SegFormer-B5 archives the best IoU of 84.0%, outperforming all existing

8

methods by at least 1.8% mIoU, and it runs 5 _×_ faster and 4 _×_ smaller than SETR [7]. On Cityscapes test set, we follow the common setting [18] and merge the validation images to the train set and report results using Imagenet-1K pre-training and also using Mapillary Vistas [74]. As reported in Table 4, using only Cityscapes fine data and Imagenet-1K pre-training, our method achieves 82.2% mIoU outperforming all other methods including SETR, which uses ImageNet-22K pre-training and the additional Cityscapes coarse data. Using Mapillary pre-training, our sets a new state-of-the-art result of 83.1% mIoU.

## **4.4 Robustness to natural corruptions**

Model robustness is important for many safety-critical tasks such as autonomous driving [75]. In this experiment, we evaluate the robustness of SegFormer to common corruptions and perturbations. To this end, we follow [75] and generate Cityscapes-C, which expands the Cityscapes validation set with 16 types of algorithmically generated corruptions from noise, blur, weather and digital categories. We compare our method to DeeplabV3+ and other methods as reported in [75]. We also compare with SETR with DeiT Transformer backbone. The results for this experiment are summarized in Table 5.

Our method significantly outperforms previous CNN-based methods, yielding a relative improvement of up to 588% on Gaussian Noise and up to 295% on snow weather. SegFormer also outperforms SETR in general except for one corruption (snow). The results indicate the strong robustness of SegFormer, which we envision to benefit safety-critical applications where robustness is important.
Table 5: **Main results on Cityscapes-C.** “DLv3+”, “MBv2”, “R” and “X” refer to DeepLabv3+, MobileNetv2, ResNet and Xception. The mIoUs of compared methods are reported from [75].

## **5 Conclusion**

In this paper, we present SegFormer, a simple, clean yet powerful semantic segmentation method which contains a positional-encoding-free, hierarchical Transformer encoder and a lightweight AllMLP decoder. It avoids common complex designs in previous methods, leading to both high efficiency and performance. SegFormer not only achieves new state of the art results on common datasets, but also shows strong zero-shot robustness. We hope our method can serve as a solid baseline for semantic segmentation and motivate further research. One potential limitation is that even our lightest model may still be too heavy for some edge devices. Thus mixed-precision training, pruning, hardware-friendly attention designs and energy consumption are important parts of our future work.

## **Broader Impact**

Efficiency, accuracy, and robustness are important aspects of AI models. Our work pushes the boundary of semantic segmentation models in these three aspects. We envision that the work will benefit a wide range of safety-critical applications, such as autonomous driving and robot navigation. The proposed method improves the “in-the-wild” robustness of these applications, ultimately leading to better safety. Despite such improvement, we fully understand this work is by no means perfect and there are still many challenges towards reliable real world application. Our models may be subject to biases and other possible undesired mistakes, depending on how they are trained in reality. Our

9

model may also be used for surveillance similar to other AI recognition methods, even though it is not mainly designed for surveillance applications.

coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3-flash-preview)
coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
SegFormer introduces a hierarchical Transformer encoder paired with a lightweight MLP decoder for semantic segmentation, emphasizing a positional-encoding-free design to handle varying image resolutions.

The paper provides a significant contribution by demonstrating that complex decoders are not strictly necessary for Transformer-based segmentation when the encoder captures multi-scale features and a large effective receptive field. The performance gains on ADE20K and Cityscapes are substantial, and the efficiency-accuracy trade-offs across the B0-B5 scale are well-documented.

Overview issues:

- Incomplete Validation of Positional-Encoding-Free Spatial Awareness: The paper claims in Section 3.1 that a 3x3 convolution in the Mix-FFN is sufficient to provide positional information, citing the 'leak location information' from zero padding. While Table 1c shows robustness to resolution changes, the paper lacks a direct test on tasks or datasets that require absolute spatial awareness (e.g., coordinate-dependent segmentation tasks). The current evaluation on Cityscapes and ADE20K mainly tests relative spatial relationships. To strengthen this claim, the authors should include a diagnostic experiment comparing the model's performance on a synthetic task where absolute pixel coordinates are the primary discriminative feature, ensuring the 'leak' is indeed a reliable substitute for explicit PE.

- Omission of Low-Data Regime Performance for MLP Decoders: Section 3.2 argues that the All-MLP decoder is superior due to its simplicity and the encoder's large effective receptive field. However, the experiments are conducted on large-scale datasets like ADE20K and Cityscapes. MLP-based heads often rely on the abundance of data to learn feature aggregation weights that more structured decoders (like ASPP or U-Net) encode through inductive biases. The paper does not address whether this lightweight design maintains its edge when training data is scarce. Including a few-shot or low-data ablation study would clarify if the MLP decoder's performance is contingent on high-volume supervision.

- Unclear Generalization of Zero-Shot Robustness Claims: In Section 4.4 and Table 5, the paper highlights 'excellent zero-shot robustness' on Cityscapes-C. While the mIoU numbers under corruption are impressive, the analysis does not explain *why* the MiT encoder provides this robustness compared to CNNs or SETR. It is unclear if the benefit stems from the hierarchical structure, the specific attention mechanism, or the removal of positional encodings. The authors should provide a more detailed breakdown or a qualitative analysis of feature stability under noise to move beyond empirical observation and provide a mechanistic explanation for this robustness.

- Limited Evaluation of Boundary Accuracy: The paper relies almost exclusively on mIoU as the primary metric in Tables 1 and 2. Semantic segmentation, particularly in high-resolution datasets like Cityscapes, often suffers from boundary blurring when using aggressive downsampling or lightweight decoders. Since SegFormer uses a 4x4 patch size and a simple MLP upsampling scheme, it might struggle with fine-grained boundary precision compared to methods with dedicated boundary refinement modules. Adding a boundary-specific metric (such as Boundary IoU or F-score) would provide a more complete picture of the trade-offs involved in using a simplified MLP decoder.

- Missing Latency Analysis on Edge Hardware: The conclusion (Section 5) mentions that the B0 model might still be too heavy for some edge devices, yet the speed benchmarks in Table 2 are performed on a Tesla V100. For a paper emphasizing 'efficiency' and 'real-time applications,' the lack of latency measurements on mobile or embedded platforms (e.g., Jetson or mobile CPUs) is a significant omission. The FLOPs count is a proxy for complexity but does not always correlate with actual runtime on resource-constrained hardware where memory bandwidth often bottlenecks MLP layers. Providing benchmarks on at least one edge-tier device would substantiate the claim that SegFormer is suitable for the 'safety-critical' edge applications mentioned in the Broader Impact.

- Missing verification of the sequence reduction ratio impact: The paper introduces a sequence reduction process in Section 3.1 to reduce self-attention complexity from quadratic to linear relative to a reduction ratio R. While the authors state they set R to [64, 16, 4, 1] across the four stages, they provide no ablation study or sensitivity analysis for these specific values. This is a significant gap because the choice of R directly dictates the 'efficiency' claim that is central to the paper's title. Readers cannot determine if the performance-efficiency frontier is sensitive to these hyperparameters or if a different schedule would better balance local and global context. To complete this, the authors should include a table comparing mIoU and GFLOPs for SegFormer-B0 using alternative R schedules, such as [32, 16, 8, 4] or a constant R across stages.

- Incomplete recovery of the ViT baseline as a special case: The paper positions SegFormer as a superior alternative to ViT-based architectures like SETR, yet it does not formally demonstrate that its hierarchical encoder can recover or surpass the performance of a flat ViT when the hierarchy is collapsed. Since the MiT encoder uses overlapped patch merging and Mix-FFN, it is unclear if the gains over SETR (Table 2) come from the hierarchical multi-scale output or these specific architectural tweaks. The paper would benefit from a controlled experiment where the MiT encoder is configured to produce a single-scale feature map (e.g., by setting strides to 1 after the first stage) to isolate the benefit of the hierarchical structure itself. This would clarify whether the 'hierarchical' claim is the primary driver of the reported 1.6% mIoU improvement on ADE20K.

- Missing implementation guidance for the All-MLP decoder fusion: Section 3.2 describes the All-MLP decoder as fusing multi-level features, but it lacks a discussion on how to handle the significant discrepancy in semantic density between Stage-1 and Stage-4 features. Equation 4 shows a simple concatenation and linear projection, which assumes that the 1/4 resolution features from early layers and the 1/32 resolution features from late layers are equally informative for the final mask. In practice, early layers in Transformers often contain low-level noise that can degrade segmentation if not gated. The paper should provide a visualization of the learned weights in the 'Linear(4C, C)' fusion layer to show which stages the model prioritizes for different object scales. Without this, the 'simplicity' of the decoder remains a black box that may be difficult for practitioners to tune for new datasets.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include a diagnostic experiment or discussion on the limitations of zero-padding as a source of absolute spatial information.

- Add boundary-specific accuracy metrics (e.g., trimap IoU or Boundary IoU) to the Cityscapes results to evaluate the MLP decoder's precision.

- Provide a mechanistic analysis or visualization of feature maps under corruption to explain the observed zero-shot robustness.

- Report latency/FPS on an edge-tier hardware platform to support the real-time deployment claims.

comments.title: Inconsistent Cityscapes mIoU reporting
comments.quote: d. Our best model, SegFormer-B5, achieves  $84.0\%$  mIoU on Cityscapes validation set and shows excellent zero-shot robustness on Cityscapes-C. Code
comments.explanation: The abstract claims 84.0% mIoU for SegFormer-B5 on the Cityscapes validation set, but Section 4.3 and Table 4 report 83.1% when using ImageNet-1K pre-training. While Table 1a shows 84.0% for multi-scale (MS) testing, the abstract presents this figure without qualification. This is misleading because the 84.0% result relies on a specific inference technique (MS) that is not the default for the other reported metrics. The text should consistently specify whether it is reporting single-scale or multi-scale results to ensure fair comparisons.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in complexity reduction claim
comments.quote: As a result, the complexity of the self-attention mechanism is reduced from $O(N^{2})$ to $O(\frac{N^{2}}{R})$. In our experiments, we set $R$ to [64, 16, 4, 1] from stage-1 to stage-4.
comments.explanation: The claim that complexity is reduced to $O(N^2/R)$ is technically correct regarding the constant factor, but it is misleading to frame this as a solution to quadratic complexity. Since $R$ is a fixed hyperparameter, the asymptotic complexity remains $O(N^2)$ with respect to the sequence length $N$. This is particularly evident in Stage 4 where $R=1$, meaning the complexity is exactly $O(N^2)$. The paper should clarify that the reduction is a constant factor improvement per stage rather than an asymptotic change to linear complexity.

comments.title: Inaccurate relative improvement calculations for robustness
comments.quote: thod significantly outperforms previous CNN-based methods, yielding a relative improvement of up to  $588\%$  on Gaussian Noise and up to  $295\%$  on snow weather.
comments.explanation: The 588% and 295% relative improvement claims are mathematically inconsistent with the data in Table 5. For Gaussian Noise, comparing SegFormer-B5 (57.8 mIoU) to the strongest CNN baseline (DLv3+ X41 at 17.0 mIoU) yields a 240% improvement. Comparing it to the weakest (GSCNN at 5.5 mIoU) yields 950%. Similarly, for snow, the improvement over the best CNN (19.3 mIoU) is 110.8%. The 588% and 295% figures do not correspond to any standard delta between the proposed model and the baselines. These values must be recalculated and verified.

comments.title: Contradiction regarding resolution-independent performance
comments.quote: As a result, our encoder can easily adapt to arbitrary test resolutions without impacting the performance.
comments.explanation: The claim that the encoder adapts to arbitrary resolutions 'without impacting the performance' is inaccurate. While removing positional encoding (PE) avoids the specific degradation caused by PE interpolation, changing the test resolution still alters the effective receptive field and object scales relative to the training distribution. Table 1c actually demonstrates this impact, showing a drop from 80.5% to 79.8% mIoU when changing resolution. The text should be revised to state that it avoids the interpolation-induced performance drop specifically.

comments.title: Inconsistent training iterations for COCO-Stuff
comments.quote: We trained the models using AdamW optimizer for 160K iterations on ADE20K, Cityscapes, and 80K iterations on COCO-Stuff.
comments.explanation: The training schedule for COCO-Stuff (80K iterations) is half as long as that for ADE20K (160K), despite COCO-Stuff having nearly six times more training images. With a batch size of 16, 80K iterations covers only about 10 epochs of COCO-Stuff, whereas 160K iterations on ADE20K covers over 100 epochs. This suggests the COCO-Stuff models are significantly undertrained compared to the other benchmarks, which may undermine the validity of the cross-dataset performance comparisons.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameter counts for B5 decoder
comments.quote: For MiT-B5 encoder, the decoder only takes up to 4% of the total number of parameters in the model.
comments.explanation: There is a numerical discrepancy regarding the MiT-B5 decoder parameters. Table 1a lists the MiT-B5 encoder at 81.4M and the decoder at 3.3M, totaling 84.7M. However, Table 3 lists the MLP decoder for MiT-B5 as having 3.3M parameters while the ViT-L MLP decoder has 0.6M. If the decoder scales with the channel dimension C as described in Table 1b, and C=768 is used for B2 through B5, the decoder size should be consistent. The jump from 0.6M (B1) to 3.3M (B2) and then remaining constant for B5 needs explicit confirmation in the text.

comments.title: Inversion of hierarchical feature terminology
comments.quote: SegFormer’s encoder has a hierarchical architecture, which is smaller than ViT and can capture both high-resolution coarse and low-resolution fine features.
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly associates 'high-resolution' with 'coarse' features and 'low-resolution' with 'fine' features. In standard computer vision hierarchies, early stages produce high-resolution features that capture fine-grained spatial details, while later stages produce low-resolution features that capture coarse semantic information. This inversion of terms contradicts the technical description in Section 3.1 and should be corrected to avoid confusing the reader.

comments.title: Ambiguous evaluation rescaling strategy
comments.quote: During evaluation, we rescale the short side of the image to training cropping size and keep the aspect ratio for ADE20K and COCO-Stuff.
comments.explanation: This evaluation strategy is mathematically ambiguous. If the short side is rescaled to 512 while maintaining the aspect ratio, the long side will exceed 512, creating an input resolution that differs from the training crop. The paper should clarify how the MLP decoder handles these variable long-side dimensions during inference, specifically whether it uses global pooling or if the linear layers are applied to variable-sized feature maps.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in concatenation formula
comments.quote: F = \operatorname {L i n e a r} (4 C, C) (\operatorname {C o n c a t} (\hat {F} _ {i})), \forall i
comments.explanation: The use of the universal quantifier $\forall i$ in the definition of $F$ is mathematically incorrect. The variable $i$ indexes the four distinct feature levels ($i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$) being aggregated. The concatenation operation consumes all $i$ to produce a single fused feature map $F$. Applying $\forall i$ implies there are four different versions of $F$, which contradicts the subsequent step where a single $M$ is derived from $F$.

comments.title: Ambiguity in final mask resolution
comments.quote: Finally, another MLP layer takes the fused feature to predict the segmentation mask  $M$  with a  $\frac{H}{4} \times \frac{W}{4} \times N_{cls}$  resolution
comments.explanation: The description of the decoder stops at $H/4 \times W/4$ resolution. However, standard benchmarks like Cityscapes require predictions at the original $H \times W$ resolution. While bilinear upsampling is common, the text should explicitly mention this final step to be technically complete, as the current description implies the model's final output is at a reduced resolution.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
SegFormer presents a compelling and well-motivated architectural design for semantic segmentation, combining a hierarchical Transformer encoder without positional encoding and a lightweight MLP decoder. The paper demonstrates strong performance on standard benchmarks with favorable efficiency metrics, and its ablation studies provide useful insights into design choices. The emphasis on simplicity, efficiency, and robustness aligns well with practical deployment needs, particularly in safety-critical domains.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient comparison with contemporary mask-based Transformer methods: The paper positions SegFormer against older Transformer-based methods like SETR and CNN-based baselines but omits comparisons with more recent and competitive approaches such as MaskFormer, Mask2Former, or SegViT, which have advanced the mask classification paradigm and set new benchmarks on ADE20K. Table 2 includes SETR but not these stronger contemporaries, despite their relevance and public availability by the time of NeurIPS 2021. This weakens the claim of state-of-the-art performance. The literature context confirms that Mask2Former, for example, outperforms SegFormer on ADE20K with better efficiency. Without such comparisons, the paper's contribution appears less novel and impactful than claimed.

- Overstated efficiency claims due to incomplete FLOPs and speed reporting: The paper reports FLOPs and FPS for full models but does not clearly break down inference time between encoder and decoder, nor does it specify whether speed measurements include preprocessing (e.g., sliding window handling on Cityscapes). For real-time claims, especially against ICNet or DeeplabV3+, this is critical. Table 2 lists SegFormer-B0 at 15.2 FPS, but without details on input resolution scaling or hardware (e.g., TensorRT), comparisons are hard to reproduce. The claim of being '5× faster than SETR' lacks context—SETR's speed is limited by its heavy decoder, but newer methods may close this gap. Efficiency claims need more granular, standardized reporting to be credible.

- Lack of validation for zero-shot robustness claim on Cityscapes-C: The paper claims 'excellent zero-shot robustness' on Cityscapes-C but provides no definition or baseline comparison for what constitutes 'excellent'. While Table 5 shows SegFormer-B5 outperforming CNNs and SETR-DeiT, it does not compare to other recent robust models or report metrics like mCE (mean corruption error) commonly used in robustness evaluation. Moreover, 'zero-shot' is misleading—no evidence is given that the model was trained on clean data only and tested on corrupted data without fine-tuning, which is standard for zero-shot robustness. The claim requires clarification and standardized evaluation to be meaningful.

- Inadequate ablation of positional encoding removal across datasets: The ablation in Table 1c supports the removal of positional encoding by showing better performance and resolution robustness on Cityscapes, but this experiment is missing for ADE20K and COCO-Stuff. Given that ADE20K has more diverse image sizes and content, the generalizability of the Mix-FFN design is not fully established. The claim that positional encoding is unnecessary hinges on this design choice being broadly effective, yet evidence is limited to one dataset. A more comprehensive evaluation across resolutions and domains is needed to justify the architectural novelty.

- Decoder design justification lacks contrast with modern alternatives: The paper argues that the MLP decoder is sufficient due to the Transformer's large receptive field, supported by ERF analysis and comparison to CNNs. However, it does not compare against simpler yet effective alternatives like linear probes or bilinear upsampling, which could test whether the MLP adds value. Table 3 compares to UperNet and MLA but not to minimal baselines. Furthermore, recent methods show that mask tokens and cross-attention can improve segmentation with little overhead—ignoring these weakens the argument that 'lightweight' implies 'optimal'. The decoder's necessity and design superiority need stronger empirical grounding.

- No comparison to mask classification paradigms: The paper claims state-of-the-art performance but omits comparisons to contemporaneous mask-based Transformer methods like MaskFormer and Mask2Former, which have redefined semantic segmentation by decoupling mask generation from classification. These models achieve higher mIoU on ADE20K with better efficiency than SegFormer, particularly due to their use of bipartite matching and learnable query mechanisms. To validate its standing, the paper should include a direct comparison in Table 2 or an appendix, reporting mIoU and FLOPs for Mask2Former using the same training and evaluation protocol. Without this, the claim of superior performance lacks context and may mislead readers about the actual SOTA.

- Zero-shot robustness claim lacks definition and baseline: The paper asserts 'excellent zero-shot robustness' on Cityscapes-C but does not define what 'zero-shot' means in this context—specifically, whether the model was trained only on clean data and evaluated on corrupted versions without fine-tuning. While Table 5 shows strong performance, it omits key baselines like RobustNet or recent adversarially trained models that set benchmarks for corruption robustness. The paper should either clarify the evaluation protocol as standard zero-shot robustness and report mean Corruption Error (mCE), or reframe the claim to 'improved robustness' with appropriate comparisons. As written, the term 'zero-shot' risks overstatement.

- Mix-FFN generalization untested on ADE20K: The ablation study in Table 1c validates the Mix-FFN design on Cityscapes under resolution shifts, but the same test is missing for ADE20K, which has greater resolution variability and more diverse scenes. To support the broader claim that positional encoding can be removed without sacrificing performance across datasets, the authors should retrain a MiT-B2 model on ADE20K using both PE and Mix-FFN variants and evaluate at multiple test resolutions (e.g., 512×512 and 640×640). Without this, the generalizability of the core architectural innovation remains dataset-limited.

- Decoder necessity unverified against minimal baselines: The paper argues the MLP decoder is effective due to the encoder's large receptive field, but it does not test whether simpler fusion strategies—such as a single linear layer or bilinear upsampling—could achieve similar results. A minimal baseline like a linear probe on concatenated multi-level features would clarify the MLP’s added value. The authors should add this ablation to Table 1d, comparing mIoU for a linear decoder versus the current MLP design. Otherwise, the claim that the 'lightweight' decoder is a key innovation rests on incomplete evidence.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include comparisons with modern mask-based Transformers (e.g., MaskFormer, Mask2Former, SegViT) on ADE20K and Cityscapes to properly contextualize performance claims.

- Provide detailed inference timing breakdowns and clarify evaluation protocols (e.g., sliding window, resolution, hardware) to support efficiency and speed claims.

- Clarify and validate the 'zero-shot robustness' claim on Cityscapes-C with standard metrics and baselines, and confirm that no corruption-aware training was used.

- Extend the positional encoding ablation to ADE20K and COCO-Stuff across multiple test resolutions to demonstrate generalizability.

- Strengthen decoder ablation by comparing against minimal baselines (e.g., linear decoder) and discussing trade-offs with modern lightweight alternatives.

comments.title: Inconsistent mIoU claims for SegFormer-B5 across sections
comments.quote: Our best model, SegFormer-B5, achieves  $84.0\%$  mIoU on Cityscapes validation set and shows excellent zero-shot robustness on Cityscapes-C.
comments.explanation: The abstract and Table 1a report 84.0% mIoU for SegFormer-B5 on Cityscapes, but Section 4.3 states the best result is 83.1% mIoU. This discrepancy is not explained and undermines confidence in the reported performance. The paper must reconcile these values—either by updating Section 4.3 to reflect 84.0% or revising the abstract and tables if 83.1% is correct. As written, the inconsistency misleads readers about the model's peak performance.

comments.title: Unsupported claim of 5× speedup over SETR
comments.quote: Our largest model, SegFormer-B5, yields 84.0% mIoU, which represents a relative 1.8% mIoU improvement while being 5 $\times$ faster than SETR *[7]*.
comments.explanation: The claim that SegFormer-B5 is '5× faster than SETR' lacks empirical support. Table 2 does not report FPS or latency for SETR, making this comparison unverifiable. While SETR is known to be slow, the paper must provide inference speed measurements under the same hardware and evaluation protocol to justify the 5× speedup. Without such data, the claim is speculative and weakens the efficiency argument.

comments.title: Implausible relative improvement percentages in robustness analysis
comments.quote: Our method significantly outperforms previous CNN-based methods, yielding a relative improvement of up to  $588\%$  on Gaussian Noise and up to  $295\%$  on snow weather.
comments.explanation: The reported '588% improvement' on Gaussian Noise is mathematically inconsistent with the data in Table 5. SegFormer-B5 achieves 57.8 mIoU versus DeepLabv3+ (X41)'s 17.0, a relative gain of ~240%, not 588%. Similarly, claiming a 295% improvement on snow is indefensible since SegFormer-B5 (40.7) underperforms SETR-DeiT (44.3) on that corruption. These figures appear miscalculated or misattributed, and the paper should either correct the percentages or specify the exact baseline used for each claim.

comments.title: Contradictory statement on corruption performance vs. SETR
comments.quote: SegFormer also outperforms SETR in general except for one corruption (snow).
comments.explanation: This claim conflicts with the earlier assertion of a '295% improvement on snow weather'—a corruption where SegFormer-B5 actually performs worse than SETR-DeiT (40.7 vs 44.3). The paper cannot simultaneously claim superior performance 'except on snow' and report a large relative gain on snow. This contradiction suggests either a mislabeling of the corruption type or an error in computing relative improvements. The results must be reconciled to avoid misleading readers.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated state-of-the-art claim without comparison to contemporaries
comments.quote: Moreover, SegFormer-B5 outperforms all other approaches, including the previous best SETR, and establishes a new state-of-the-art of  $51.8\%$ , which is  $1.6\%$  mIoU better than SETR while being significantly more efficient.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'new state-of-the-art' on ADE20K is not supported by comparisons to contemporaneous methods like Mask2Former, which achieved 53.5% mIoU—over 1.7 points higher than SegFormer-B5's 51.8%. These models were public by NeurIPS 2021 and represent the actual SOTA. Omitting them weakens the novelty claim. The paper should either include these comparisons or reframe the claim to reflect competitive but not dominant performance.

comments.title: Ambiguous upsampling target in decoder formulation
comments.explanation: The notation suggests a fixed output size, but in practice, upsampling is relative to input resolution. For example, a stage-4 feature at $H/32 \times W/32$ must be upsampled by 8× to reach $H/4 \times W/4$, while stage-2 features require only 2×. The current formulation obscures this stage-dependent scaling and could mislead implementation. Clarify that the target is $1/4$ the input resolution, not an absolute grid size.

comments.title: Decoder output resolution phrasing implies fixed size
comments.quote: Finally, another MLP layer takes the fused feature to predict the segmentation mask  $M$  with a  $\frac{H}{4} \times \frac{W}{4} \times N_{cls}$  resolution, where  $N_{cls}$  is the number of categories.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'with a $\frac{H}{4} \times \frac{W}{4}$ resolution' risks misinterpretation as a fixed output grid. In reality, the output scales with input size, meaning the model produces predictions at stride 4. This is critical for resolution robustness, a key design goal. Rephrase to 'at $1/4$ the input spatial resolution' to avoid implying a fixed output size.

comments.title: Inconsistent crop sizes for ADE20K training
comments.quote: random cropping to $512\times 512$, 1024$\times 1024$, $512\times 512$ for ADE20K, Cityscapes and COCO-Stuff. Following *[9]* we set crop size to $640\times 640$ on ADE20K for our largest model B5.
comments.explanation: The text first states a 512×512 crop for ADE20K but later specifies 640×640 for B5 without clarifying whether this replaces the default or applies only to that model. This creates ambiguity about the actual training resolution across the model family. Specify that smaller models use 512×512 while B5 uses 640×640 to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Learning rate schedule description contradicts optimizer behavior
comments.quote: The learning rate was set to an initial value of 0.00006 and then used a “poly” LR schedule with factor 1.0 by default.
comments.explanation: A 'poly' schedule with factor 1.0 implies no decay, meaning the learning rate remains constant at 6e-5. However, this contradicts standard usage where 'factor' refers to the power parameter (e.g., power=0.9). A constant LR of 6e-5 is also unusually low for AdamW with pretrained weights. Clarify whether the schedule actually decays the LR and specify the correct power parameter.

comments.title: Decoder FLOPs values do not scale with encoder size
comments.explanation: The decoder FLOPs for UperNet are identical (210.7 GFlops) for MiT-B2 and MiT-B5, despite the latter having larger feature dimensions. This is implausible—decoder computation should increase with encoder output size. Either the values are misreported or computed under unrealistic assumptions. The paper should report FLOPs conditioned on actual feature maps or clarify the measurement protocol.

comments.title: Overstatement of zero-shot robustness without protocol clarification
comments.quote: SegFormer not only achieves new state of the art results on common datasets, but also shows strong zero-shot robustness.
comments.explanation: The term 'zero-shot robustness' is used without defining the evaluation protocol. While the setup (train on clean, test on corrupted) aligns with standard zero-shot evaluation, the paper does not report mCE or compare to robustness benchmarks. Moreover, 'strong' is a qualitative claim not grounded in comparison to dedicated robust models. Reframe as 'improved robustness under image corruptions' to match the actual experiments.

comments.title: Incomplete funding disclosure for industrial affiliation
comments.quote: Ping Luo is supported by the General Research Fund of Hong Kong No.27208720.
comments.explanation: The funding statement omits support for authors affiliated with NVIDIA, despite the work being developed at NVlabs and hosted on an NVIDIA GitHub. NeurIPS requires full disclosure of all funding sources. Either add corporate funding details or explicitly state that no other sources exist to ensure transparency.

comments.title: Misleading performance drop description in Mix-FFN ablation
comments.quote:
e accuracy drops  $3.3\%$  when using a positional encoding with a

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

lower resolution. In contrast, when we use the proposed Mix-FFN the performance drop is reduced to only  $0.7\%$
comments.explanation: The phrasing incorrectly attributes the accuracy drop to 'lower resolution', but the drop occurs when testing at *higher* resolution (1024×2048), where positional encoding interpolation fails. The current wording inverts the causal direction. Correct to: 'the accuracy drops 3.3% when using positional encoding at higher resolution (1024×2048), whereas with Mix-FFN the drop is only 0.7%.'

comments.title: Concatenation order of multi-scale features not specified
comments.quote: Then, in a second step, features are up-sampled to 1/4th and concatenated together. Third, a MLP layer is adopted to fuse the concatenated features  $F$ .
comments.explanation: The order in which multi-scale features are concatenated affects channel grouping and fusion. While common practice concatenates from coarse to fine, this is not universal, and the paper does not specify or justify the order. Add a sentence clarifying the concatenation sequence (e.g., stage 1 to stage 4) to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Typo in dataset name reduces professionalism
comments.quote: On Citysapces, our lightweight model, SegFormer-B0, without accelerated implementations such as TensorRT, yields 71.9% mIoU at 48 FPS
comments.explanation: The misspelling 'Citysapces' instead of 'Cityscapes' is a minor but noticeable error that undermines professionalism. Given the correct spelling appears elsewhere, this is an isolated typo. Correct it to maintain consistency and clarity.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper presents SegFormer, a semantic segmentation framework combining hierarchical Transformer encoders with lightweight MLP decoders. The authors claim significant improvements in efficiency, accuracy, and robustness over state-of-the-art methods. While the empirical results are impressive, there are several methodological concerns that need to be addressed regarding the validation of architectural claims, completeness of ablation studies, and generalizability of results.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by proposing a simplified yet effective Transformer-based segmentation architecture. The empirical results showing state-of-the-art performance with significantly fewer parameters are compelling. The work addresses important practical concerns like resolution robustness and computational efficiency. However, the methodological validation has some gaps that weaken the strength of the claims about why the proposed design choices work.

Overview issues:

- Incomplete validation of positional encoding removal: The paper claims that removing positional encoding (PE) and using Mix-FFN improves performance and resolution robustness (Section 3.1, Table 1c). However, the comparison is limited to only two test resolutions on Cityscapes. This is insufficient to validate the universal claim that removing PE is beneficial across all scenarios. The paper should test a wider range of resolution mismatches and include more comprehensive baselines like CPVT's conditional PE or learnable PE schemes. Without this broader evaluation, the claim that PE removal is universally advantageous remains inadequately supported.

- MLP decoder expressiveness not sufficiently analyzed: While the paper shows the MLP decoder works well with Transformer encoders (Table 1d), it doesn't thoroughly investigate the limitations of this design. The ERF analysis in Section 3.2 is qualitative, and there's no systematic study of when the MLP decoder might fail. The paper should include experiments showing performance degradation scenarios or comparisons with more expressive decoders on challenging cases. Without understanding the boundaries of the MLP decoder's effectiveness, readers cannot assess its suitability for different applications.

- Missing ablation for hierarchical vs. non-hierarchical design: The paper emphasizes the importance of hierarchical multi-scale features but doesn't isolate this factor from other innovations. There's no experiment comparing a hierarchical Transformer with PE vs. a non-hierarchical one without PE, or vice versa. This makes it difficult to attribute performance gains specifically to the hierarchical design. A proper ablation should systematically vary hierarchical structure and PE presence to quantify their individual contributions to the final performance.

- Limited robustness evaluation scope: The robustness evaluation in Section 4.4 only covers Cityscapes-C corruptions and lacks comparison with recent Transformer-based methods beyond SETR. The claim of "excellent zero-shot robustness" is too strong given this limited evaluation. The paper should include additional robustness benchmarks like ImageNet-C adaptation for segmentation, natural distribution shifts, and comparisons with more recent Transformer segmentation methods. Without broader robustness testing, the generalization claims remain unsubstantiated.

- Efficiency metrics lack comprehensive analysis: The paper reports FLOPs and FPS but doesn't provide detailed memory usage analysis or latency across different hardware platforms. The efficiency claims in Table 2 compare FPS at different input resolutions, making direct comparisons difficult. A more thorough efficiency evaluation should include memory consumption, inference speed on multiple hardware configurations (CPU, different GPUs), and energy consumption metrics. Without this, the claimed efficiency advantages cannot be properly assessed for practical deployment scenarios.

- Missing demonstration of MLP decoder failure modes: The paper shows the MLP decoder works well with Transformer encoders but never demonstrates its limitations. Readers need to see cases where this simple decoder fails to understand the trade-offs. The authors should include experiments on challenging segmentation scenarios where the MLP decoder underperforms, such as handling fine-grained boundaries or small objects. For instance, they could test on the COCO-Stuff dataset's small object categories or construct synthetic examples with thin structures. Without showing where the approach breaks down, the claim of universal effectiveness remains unconvincing.

- Incomplete cross-resolution generalization analysis: The paper's claim that removing positional encoding enables better cross-resolution performance is only tested on two resolutions (768×768 and 1024×2048) on Cityscapes. This is insufficient to support the broader claim. The authors should evaluate across a more diverse set of resolution ratios, including both upscaling and downscaling scenarios. Testing on datasets with different native resolutions like ADE20K and COCO-Stuff would strengthen the argument. Additionally, they should compare against CPVT's conditional positional encoding approach under the same resolution mismatches to properly contextualize their contribution.

- No worked example of hierarchical feature contribution: The paper emphasizes the importance of hierarchical multi-scale features but never provides a concrete example showing how different scales contribute to segmentation quality. A visualization or quantitative analysis of which scales matter most for different object sizes would help readers understand the mechanism. For example, they could compute the contribution of each scale (1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32) to segmentation performance on objects of different sizes in ADE20K, or show failure cases when removing specific scales. Without this analysis, the benefit of the hierarchical design remains abstract.

- Missing practical deployment guidance: Despite claiming efficiency advantages, the paper provides no practical guidance for deploying SegFormer in real applications. There's no analysis of memory footprint during inference, latency on different hardware (CPU, mobile GPUs), or optimization strategies for edge devices. The authors should include benchmark results on at least one mobile platform and discuss model quantization or pruning potential. Without this implementation guidance, the claimed efficiency advantages cannot be translated into practice, especially given their acknowledgment that even B0 may be too heavy for some edge devices.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Expand positional encoding ablation with more resolutions and alternative PE schemes

- Add systematic analysis of MLP decoder limitations and failure cases

- Include proper ablation study separating hierarchical design from PE removal effects

- Broaden robustness evaluation beyond Cityscapes-C with more recent baselines

- Provide comprehensive efficiency metrics including memory usage and multi-platform latency

comments.title: Performance inconsistency between abstract and main text
comments.quote: Our best model, SegFormer-B5, achieves  $84.0\%$  mIoU on Cityscapes validation set
comments.explanation: The abstract claims SegFormer-B5 achieves 84.0% mIoU on Cityscapes validation set, but Section 4.3 reports 83.1% mIoU. This 0.9% discrepancy needs clarification - either the abstract number is incorrect, or there's an unexplained difference in evaluation protocols. The abstract should match the reported results throughout the paper.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated efficiency comparison
comments.quote: SegFormer-B4 achieves  $50.3\%$  mIoU on ADE20K with 64M parameters, being  $5\times$  smaller and  $2.2\%$  better than the previous best method.
comments.explanation: The claim of being "5× smaller" lacks context - smaller than what specifically? If referring to parameters, this would imply the previous best method had ~320M parameters, but no baseline is named. Without specifying which method is being compared to and its exact parameter count, this efficiency claim cannot be verified. The abstract should either name the specific baseline method being compared against or remove this quantitative comparison.

comments.title: Incorrect DETR citation attribution
comments.quote: DETR [50] is the first to use Transformers for end-to-end object detection framework without non-maximum suppression (NMS).
comments.explanation: The citation [50] incorrectly attributes DETR to the same reference as DeiT. DETR was introduced in 'End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers' by Carion et al. (2020), while DeiT (Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation) by Touvron et al. (2020) is a different paper about efficient ViT training. These are distinct works with different contributions and should have separate citations.

comments.title: Incorrect universal quantifier in feature fusion equation
comments.quote:
$$
F = \operatorname {L i n e a r} (4 C, C) (\operatorname {C o n c a t} (\hat {F} _ {i})), \forall i
$$
comments.explanation: The universal quantifier "∀i" is mathematically inconsistent with the Concat operation. Concatenation requires multiple inputs, but as written with "∀i", it suggests applying to each individual \hat{F}_i separately. The text describes concatenating ALL upsampled features together, then applying a single linear layer. Rewrite as "$$\nF = \operatorname {L i n e a r} (4 C, C) (\operatorname {C o n c a t} (\hat {F} _ {1}, \hat {F} _ {2}, \hat {F} _ {3}, \hat {F} _ {4}))\n$$" because the concatenation and linear fusion are applied to all features simultaneously.

comments.title: Missing specification of upsampling method
comments.quote: $$\hat{F}_i = \text{Upsample}(\frac{H}{4} \times \frac{W}{4})(\hat{F}_i), \forall i \tag{4}$$
comments.explanation: The upsampling operation is not mathematically specified. Different upsampling methods (bilinear, nearest neighbor, transposed convolution) produce different results and affect the final segmentation quality. The equation only specifies the target resolution but not the interpolation method. Add a specification after equation (4): "where Upsample denotes bilinear interpolation" or specify the exact upsampling method used.

comments.title: Inconsistent training iterations for ablation studies
comments.quote: We trained the models using AdamW optimizer for 160K iterations on ADE20K, Cityscapes, and 80K iterations on COCO-Stuff. Exceptionally, for the ablation studies, we trained the models for 40K iterations.
comments.explanation: The paper reports using 40K iterations for ablation studies while using 160K iterations for main results. This creates an unfair comparison because ablation results cannot be directly compared to main results. A 4x reduction in training iterations will likely lead to underperformance, making it impossible to determine whether observed performance differences are due to architectural changes or insufficient training. This undermines the validity of the ablation study conclusions. The authors should use consistent iteration counts or clearly state that ablation results are not directly comparable.

comments.title: Inconsistent crop size reporting for ADE20K
comments.quote: ze the decoder. During training, we applied data augmentation through random resize with ratio 0.5-2.0, random horizontal flipping, and random cropping to $512\times 512$, 1024$\times 1024$, $512\times 512$ for ADE20K, Cityscapes and COCO-Stuff. Following *[9]* we set crop size to $640\times 640$ on ADE20K for our largest
comments.explanation: The text states ADE20K uses $512\times 512$ crop size, then immediately contradicts this by saying B5 uses $640\times 640$ crop size on ADE20K. This creates ambiguity about which crop size was actually used for different models. The inconsistency makes it unclear whether performance comparisons between B5 and other models on ADE20K are fair, since different input resolutions were used. The authors should clarify whether all models except B5 used $512\times 512$ and only B5 used $640\times 640$.

comments.title: Unfair FPS comparison across different input resolutions
comments.quote: SegFormer-B0 yields 15.2 FPS and  $76.2\%$  mIoU (the shorter side of input image being 1024), which represents a  $1.3\%$  mIoU improvement and a  $2\times$  speedup compared to DeeplabV3+. Moreover, with the shorter side of input image being 512, SegFormer-B0 runs at 47.6 FPS and yields  $71.9\%$  mIoU, which is 17.3 FPS faster and  $4.2\%$  better than ICNet.
comments.explanation: The paper compares SegFormer-B0's FPS against DeeplabV3+ and ICNet without specifying the input resolutions used for the baseline methods. Since FPS scales quadratically with input resolution, these comparisons are invalid unless all methods use identical input sizes. The paper should either report baseline FPS at the same resolutions or explicitly state that comparisons are at different resolutions and acknowledge this limitation.

comments.title: Unfair comparison due to different pre-training data
comments.quote: As reported in Table 4, using only Cityscapes fine data and Imagenet-1K pre-training, our method achieves  $82.2\%$  mIoU outperforming all other methods including SETR, which uses ImageNet-22K pre-training and the additional Cityscapes coarse data.
comments.explanation: The comparison between SegFormer (ImageNet-1K pre-trained) and SETR (ImageNet-22K + coarse data pre-trained) is not fair. ImageNet-22K contains ~14M images vs ImageNet-1K's ~1.3M images, and coarse data provides additional supervision. The paper should either report SETR's performance with ImageNet-1K pre-training only or report SegFormer's performance with ImageNet-22K pre-training for a direct comparison.

comments.title: Incorrect relative improvement calculation
comments.quote: Our method significantly outperforms previous CNN-based methods, yielding a relative improvement of up to  $588\%$  on Gaussian Noise and up to  $295\%$  on snow weather.
comments.explanation: The relative improvement calculations appear incorrect. For Gaussian Noise, the best CNN method (GSCNN) scores 5.5% mIoU while SegFormer-B5 scores 57.8%. The relative improvement should be (57.8-5.5)/5.5 = 951%, not 588%. For snow weather, the best CNN method (DLv3+ X71) scores 18.8% while SegFormer-B5 scores 40.7%. The relative improvement should be (40.7-18.8)/18.8 = 116%, not 295%. The authors should recalculate these percentages.

comments.title: Contradiction in feature description
comments.quote: These features provide both high-resolution coarse features and low-resolution fine-grained features that boost the performance of semantic segmentation.
comments.explanation: The description contradicts the mathematical formulation. For $i\in\{1,2,3,4\}$, resolution $\frac{H}{2^{i+1}}\times\frac{W}{2^{i+1}}$ decreases as $i$ increases, while $C_i$ increases. Higher resolution features (smaller $i$) have fewer channels, making them "fine" rather than "coarse", and lower resolution features (larger $i$) have more channels, making them "coarse" rather than "fine-grained". Rewrite as "These features provide both high-resolution fine features and low-resolution coarse features" to match the mathematical formulation.

comments.title: Incorrect sequence reduction notation
comments.quote: $$\begin{aligned} \hat{K} &= \text{Reshape}(\frac{N}{R}, C \cdot R)(K) \\ K &= \text{Linear}(C \cdot R, C)(\hat{K}) \end{aligned}$$ (2)
comments.explanation: The notation creates a circular definition where $K$ is used to define $\hat{K}$, then $K$ is immediately redefined using $\hat{K}$. This is mathematically inconsistent as the same symbol represents both the input and output of the reduction process. Rewrite equation (2) as "$$\begin{aligned} \hat{K} &= \text{Reshape}(\frac{N}{R}, C \cdot R)(K) \\ K_{reduced} &= \text{Linear}(C \cdot R, C)(\hat{K}) \end{aligned}$$ (2)" to clearly distinguish between the original key sequence $K$ and the reduced version $K_{reduced}$.

comments.title: Inconsistent patch merging description
comments.quote: This can easily be extended to unify a $2\times 2\times C_{i}$ feature path into a $1\times 1\times C_{i+1}$ vector to obtain hierarchical feature maps. Using this, we can shrink our hierarchical features from $F_{1}$ ($\frac{H}{4}\times\frac{W}{4}\times C_{1}$) to $F_{2}$ ($\frac{H}{8}\times\frac{W}{8}\times C_{2}$)
comments.explanation: The dimensional inconsistency is confirmed. A $2\times 2$ patch merging reduces spatial dimensions by factor 2, but the text claims reduction from $\frac{H}{4}\times\frac{W}{4}$ to $\frac{H}{8}\times\frac{W}{8}$, which is factor 4. To achieve this reduction, $4\times 4$ patch merging is required. Rewrite as "This can easily be extended to unify a $4\times 4\times C_{i}$ feature patch into a $1\times 1\times C_{i+1}$ vector" to match the stated resolution reduction.

comments.title: Complexity reduction claim needs justification
comments.quote: As a result, the complexity of the self-attention mechanism is reduced from $O(N^{2})$ to $O(\frac{N^{2}}{R})$.
comments.explanation: The complexity reduction claim is not fully justified. After sequence reduction, the attention computation becomes $\text{Softmax}(\frac{QK_{reduced}^{\mathsf{T}}}{\sqrt{d_{head}}})V$, where $Q$ and $V$ have dimensions $N\times C$ but $K_{reduced}$ has dimensions $\frac{N}{R}\times C$. The matrix multiplication $QK_{reduced}^{\mathsf{T}}$ yields an $N\times\frac{N}{R}$ matrix. The complexity is actually $O(N\cdot\frac{N}{R}\cdot C) = O(\frac{N^{2}C}{R})$, not $O(\frac{N^{2}}{R})$. Add clarification: "where the complexity is $O(\frac{N^{2}C}{R})$ due to the $N\times\frac{N}{R}$ attention matrix computation".
